title: Why Toxic Resilience Is Keeping You Stuck (And What to Do Instead)
guestBio: Dr. Katie Pritchett is the founder of KPI Coaching, a leadership development and organizational consulting firm. With a PhD in experiential learning from UT Austin and years coaching Fortune 500 executives, she specializes in helping people and companies grow from the inside out through understanding the neuroscience of leadership.
excerpt: What if the resilience you've been praised for is actually keeping you trapped? Dr. Katie Pritchett reveals why women must stop wearing burnout as a badge of honor and start choosing regeneration instead.
metaDescription: Dr. Katie Pritchett breaks down toxic resilience, why women need to lead AI, and how to break generational patterns for better leadership.
keyTakeaways: Toxic resilience means wearing burnout as a badge of honor instead of recognizing it as a signal for change.
Regeneration is more powerful than resilience because it allows things to break so you can build something better.
Leadership is neuroscience—you need to understand how your people are wired to lead them effectively.
Women must be involved in creating AI technology because only we can automate the invisible labor we see and do.
Your childhood survival strategies may be running your adult leadership in ways that no longer serve you.
Breaking generational cycles means choosing to inherit healing rather than pain for the next generation.
Real transformation happens through experiential learning, not just consuming more information.

pullQuotes.text: I don't want him to inherit my pain—I want him to inherit my healing
Your patterns were inherited, but your power is not

timestamps.label: The moment Katie decided to step out on her own
Technology and human behavior: Leadership is neuroscience
How AI can give us more time to be human
Why women must lead the AI revolution
Motherhood and the switch from academia to coaching
How companies can shift from toxic productivity
Your patterns were inherited, but your power is not

content:
You've been told your entire life that resilience is a virtue. Push through. Bounce back. Keep going no matter what. But what if the very resilience you've been praised for is actually the thing keeping you stuck in cycles of burnout, overwhelm, and endless hustle?

In this powerful conversation, Dr. Katie Pritchett introduces us to a concept that will fundamentally shift how you think about strength, leadership, and what it means to truly thrive: toxic resilience. This isn't just another wellness buzzword—it's a framework that explains why so many high-achieving women find themselves exhausted, depleted, and wondering why their success doesn't feel sustainable.

The Problem with Resilience as We Know It

Dr. Katie's journey to understanding toxic resilience began with her own breaking point. As a professor at the University of Texas at Austin, she found herself working under an abusive boss whose behavior triggered deep childhood survival strategies. Instead of recognizing the toxicity of the situation, she doubled down on what she'd always been taught: resilience. Work harder. Push through. Prove your worth.

But here's what she discovered: resilience without boundaries becomes toxic. When we wear burnout as a badge of honor, when we normalize over-functioning, when we celebrate our ability to endure rather than questioning what we're enduring, we've crossed the line from healthy adaptation into toxic patterns that serve no one—not us, not our families, not our teams.

The shift happened when Dr. Katie realized she was passing these same survival strategies down to her children. The patterns that had helped her survive her childhood were now limiting her ability to thrive as an adult and as a mother. This recognition became the catalyst for her transition from academia to founding KPI Coaching, where she helps leaders understand the neuroscience behind their patterns and create sustainable change from the inside out.

Regeneration: A Revolutionary Alternative

Instead of resilience, Dr. Katie proposes something far more powerful: regeneration. While resilience asks us to bounce back to where we were, regeneration gives us permission to let things break so we can build something entirely new and better. It's the difference between patching a cracked foundation and building on solid ground.

This concept becomes especially crucial when we consider the invisible labor that women carry. You know exactly what this looks like—the mental load of remembering everyone's schedules, the emotional labor of managing family dynamics, the administrative tasks that somehow always fall to you. Regeneration means we don't just become more resilient at carrying this load; we question the distribution of the load itself.

Why Women Must Lead the AI Revolution

One of the most compelling arguments Dr. Katie makes is about women's critical role in shaping artificial intelligence. This isn't just about representation or fairness—it's about functionality. Only women can automate what only women see. The invisible labor that women have been doing for generations needs to be understood and valued before it can be systematized and, where appropriate, automated.

Think about it: AI is being developed primarily by people who have never managed a household, coordinated carpools, or juggled the complex web of relationships and responsibilities that make families and communities function. When women aren't in these rooms, entire categories of human experience remain invisible to the technology that will shape our future.

Dr. Katie envisions a world where AI handles the transactional, repetitive tasks that drain our energy, freeing us to do the deeply human work of creativity, connection, and complex problem-solving. But this future only becomes reality if women are actively involved in creating it.

Leadership as Neuroscience, Not Magic

Perhaps one of the most practical insights from this conversation is Dr. Katie's assertion that leadership isn't rocket science—it's neuroscience. Understanding how people are wired, what triggers their survival responses, and how to create psychological safety isn't intuitive; it's learnable.

This is where her background in experiential learning becomes so valuable. Real transformation doesn't happen through consuming more information—it happens through experience that rewires our neural pathways. You can read about leadership all day long, but until you practice new behaviors in real situations and feel the difference in your body and relationships, nothing actually changes.

For women in leadership positions, this understanding is revolutionary. So many of us have been trying to lead while carrying the weight of our own unhealed patterns, wondering why our teams aren't responding the way we hope. When we understand that our childhood survival strategies might be showing up as adult leadership liabilities, we can begin to make conscious choices about how we want to show up.

Breaking Generational Cycles

The most powerful moment in this conversation comes when Dr. Katie shares her intention for her children: "I don't want him to inherit my pain—I want him to inherit my healing." This single statement encapsulates what it means to be a cycle breaker, not just in our families but in our organizations and communities.

Your patterns were inherited, but your power is not. The survival strategies that your nervous system learned in childhood were adaptive then, but they may be limiting you now. The beautiful thing about understanding this is that it removes shame from the equation. There's nothing wrong with you—you're just operating from outdated programming that served you once but doesn't serve you now.

This work of breaking generational cycles isn't just personal—it's leadership development at the highest level. When you heal your own patterns, you create space for others to do the same. When you model healthy boundaries instead of toxic resilience, you give permission for your team, your family, and your community to do likewise.

The Path Forward

The invitation Dr. Katie offers isn't to become more resilient in the traditional sense. It's to become more conscious, more intentional, and more willing to let outdated structures break so something healthier can emerge. This takes courage, especially for women who have been rewarded for their ability to endure, to manage, to keep everything running smoothly.

But here's what's possible on the other side of this work: leadership that energizes rather than depletes you, relationships built on authentic connection rather than performance, and a legacy of healing rather than survival. The future needs women who understand both the technology and the humanity required to create systems that truly serve everyone.

Your patterns were inherited, but your power—your choice to regenerate rather than just survive—is entirely your own.

quiz.title: What's Your Resilience Style?
quiz.description: Are you thriving with healthy boundaries, or is your resilience actually keeping you stuck? Discover your unique pattern and learn how to build sustainable strength.

questions.question: When you're facing a particularly challenging week at work, what's your go-to response?

options.text: I assess what's truly urgent, delegate what I can, and make sure I'm getting enough sleep to handle it well
I notice my old urge to just power through, but I'm trying to be more intentional about asking for help
I buckle down and get it done—I can handle more than most people think
I feel overwhelmed and know I need a different approach, but I'm not sure what that looks like yet

questions.question: How do you typically view your childhood experiences in relation to your current leadership style?

options.text: I've done work to understand how my past shaped me, and I consciously choose which patterns to keep or change
I'm starting to see connections between my childhood survival strategies and how I lead today
My challenging experiences made me stronger and more capable than I would have been otherwise
I sense there are patterns from my past affecting me now, but I haven't fully explored that connection

questions.question: When someone compliments your ability to 'handle anything,' how do you feel?

options.text: Grateful, but I also make sure they know it's because I've learned to set good boundaries and ask for support
A mix of pride and exhaustion—I appreciate it but I'm questioning if this is sustainable
Proud and energized—I've worked hard to develop that reputation
Conflicted—part of me likes the recognition, but another part feels trapped by the expectation

questions.question: What's your relationship with rest and downtime?

options.text: I prioritize it as essential fuel for my effectiveness and creativity
I'm learning to see it as productive rather than lazy, though it still feels challenging sometimes
I'll rest when the important work is done—there's always so much that needs my attention
I know I need more of it, but I struggle with guilt or feeling like there's always something more urgent

questions.question: How do you handle it when systems or situations in your life aren't working?

options.text: I look for the root cause and am willing to rebuild things completely if that's what's needed
I'm getting better at questioning whether I should fix it or if the system itself needs to change
I find ways to work around the problems and make things function, even if it requires extra effort from me
I often feel frustrated because I know something should change, but I default to just managing the dysfunction

questions.question: When you think about the example you're setting for other women or your children, what comes up?

options.text: I want to model that strength includes knowing your limits and honoring your needs
I'm working to break cycles I inherited and show that there are healthier ways to be successful
I want them to see that you can overcome anything if you're determined and work hard enough
I worry I might be modeling patterns I don't want to pass on, but I'm not sure how to change them

results.title: The Regenerative Leader
results.description: You understand that true strength comes from knowing when to rest, reset, and rebuild. You've learned to see challenges as opportunities for growth rather than tests of endurance. Your approach to leadership honors both your needs and your team's wellbeing. This episode will give you even more tools to model this regenerative approach and help others break free from toxic resilience patterns.

results.title: The Conscious Rebuilder
results.description: You're in the beautiful process of recognizing old patterns that no longer serve you. You've started to question whether pushing through everything is actually helping or hurting your goals. Your awareness is your superpower, even if the change feels messy right now. This episode will validate your journey and give you the neuroscience behind why breaking these patterns is so challenging—and so necessary.

results.title: The Determined Achiever
results.description: Your drive and commitment have taken you far, but you're starting to feel the cost of always being 'on.' You pride yourself on handling whatever comes your way, yet something whispers that there might be a more sustainable path. Your strength is real, but it deserves better boundaries. This episode will help you understand how to channel that incredible energy in ways that fuel you instead of depleting you.

results.title: The Breakthrough Seeker
results.description: You know something needs to change, but you're not sure exactly what or how. You feel caught between old patterns of pushing through and a growing desire for something more sustainable. Your intuition is guiding you toward transformation, even when the path isn't clear. This episode will give you the framework to understand what toxic resilience looks like and concrete steps to move toward regenerative strength instead.

transcript:
Aimee: Hi everyone and welcome back to the manager, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur and mama of six. Today's guest is someone whose work sits at the intersection of humanity, leadership and innovation. 

Dr. Katie Pritchett is the founder of KPI Coaching, a leadership development and organizational consulting firm helping people and companies grow from the inside out. Before launching her own practice, Katie spent years as a professor at the business school at the University of Texas at Austin, where she specialized in experiential learning, helping people learn not just through theory, but through lived experience. She's since brought that same approach to her work with Fortune 500 companies, coaching leaders on the people side of transformation and change.

She's a mother, a writer, and a voice for what she calls toxic resilience - the culture of overfunctioning and burnout that we've normalized in modern workplaces. Her upcoming book dives deep into this concept and asks a powerful question: What if resilience isn't about pushing through? What if it's about knowing when to rest?

And what I love most, Katie is exploring how we can use AI not to replace humanity, but to return us to it. When we automate the mundane, she says we can make more space for what makes us truly human: creativity, connection, and community. Hey Katie, it's nice to have you here today.

Katie: What an intro. I really appreciate that. It's so good to be here.

Aimee: Yay. I guess we should start. You've had such a dynamic career from academia to entrepreneurship to coaching Fortune 500 executives. What was the moment that made you say it's time for me to step out on my own?

Katie: It's a good question. I've always had a side gig, a side hustle, which I feel like a lot of women can relate to, and I really appreciate you saying stepping out because that's what it felt like. It felt like stepping out onto a glass balcony 93 stories up. I had proven myself that I could do it, right? I just wanted to make it my full time.

And I think it just took that moment of - I was by all accounts externally thriving, corporate promotion, all these teaching awards, but I was hurting internally and exacerbated by a toxic boss, an abusive boss to be more clear. And what I realized was old patterns I thought I had shed many, many moons ago. I wasn't being the professional I know I am in my community, the leader at home, all those things. I was being 8 years old again. And that's because I had an abusive father. And so those triggers were coming back up.

And I realized that no matter all the work you do, which I had been doing plenty, my childhood survival strategies had taken over into this adult leadership. So I did something about it at that company. I built something different. I rewired. And when that worked for me, when you do your work or you do something, you almost can't unsee it in other people.

Aimee: Yes. I totally know. You know, I do. I know. I've done my work and I'm like, "Oh, I see it." Yeah. You become self-aware of something and then you're like, "Oh, I can't help but see it somewhere else."

Katie: Correct. And it's like what you do. I really believe your work is so important. When you teach women about invisible work, they can't unsee it anymore. It's not invisible anymore. That's how it was for me. I'm like, "Well, I have developed a medicine, a protocol. I've developed - I know what this transformation really feels like intimately, and I keep seeing it in so many other ways. How can I do this?" Almost like I couldn't not do it anymore. I'm like, I need to be able to go out and support organizations and leaders in this way.

Aimee: I love it. I love it. Yeah. You got to do what you got to do and you know when it's time you're just like there's no not doing it when it's time to do something. So you have a PhD in experiential learning which is so cool. Can you explain what that means and how it shapes your approach to coaching and leadership development?

Katie: Yes, that was my dissertation topic. That was my research topic for my PhD. And what I understand is that transformation, whether it's an individual human's transformation or we're doing an organizational transformation, it happens through experience. It's not just through information, right? We could read all the manuals in the world. But until you start building those Lego blocks yourself and make some mistakes and have to undo them, that learning doesn't happen.

I know that transformation requires direct experience, not just intellectual understanding. So I studied service learning and experiential learning and how proximate experiences create that deeper change. When I was in academia, so I taught at UT Austin for almost 10 years at the undergrad, MBA, executive ed level. And I would turn every classroom into a living lab.

I wanted students no matter what level to work directly to apply that information. So we partnered with startups and nonprofits and they had to - we'd accelerate the learning or I call it the acquisition of knowledge at the beginning of the semester and we focused the rest of the semester on the application of the knowledge so that we could see it in real time and fail and be like oh that didn't work so well and really ultimately what happens is that creates more confidence. Even if you don't do it right the first time, you have more confidence to not do it that way the next time.

And then a lot of people are confused. So you went to academia, then you went to corporate and then you have your own. And that's why I trademarked what I do with KPI coaching as keynote labs because it's that inspirational message you'd get of a keynote if you went to a conference or something. But the lab, the experiential components help you realize you can't think your way into a new behavior. You have to embody it.

Aimee: Yes. I've been thinking about this a lot because this comes up a lot on the podcast. How AI can give us answers but it can't give us the experience of discovering the answer. It doesn't give us the process of how we got to the answer. So a lot of times this is where it's lacking. It is in the discovery process that our brains uptake the information to a deeper level. And I think you're exactly right. If you're going to teach someone how to do something, you really have to feel the pain of doing something. What did you do right? What do you do wrong? Where can you improve? But it's the process of discovery that is really where we deeply learn and uptake knowledge.

Katie: Yes. If you don't mind, I would say not only the process, but the discomfort.

Aimee: Yes. And I was talking to a friend who has a teenager and she was just saying he puts all his homework into AI and she's like I'm just feeling like he's not able to explain why or how he got there. And I'm like he's not going to be able to if there's something about that discomfort and then testing and learning I think that is still a really important part of our human experience.

Katie: Yeah. My son came home from a baseball game on Sunday and his head was down and I'm like what's wrong dude? And he's like, "Oh, I made three errors in the game and I'm really disappointed in myself." And I said, "Well, did you learn from them? Do you know you won't make that mistake again? Did you figure out what you did wrong?" Because he's only been playing for two years, but he's 16, so he's older.

And he's very talented. He's got a natural gift for it, but he doesn't have a lot of the baseball IQ that other kids on his team have. So for him, I said, "Well, the good news is you're not in the major leagues. And this is where you make the mistakes so that you don't make them again. This is where you get your baseball IQ. So you learn from the pain, and in life you do. You learn from the pain."

Aimee: Good job, mama. But as a mom, isn't it so painful to watch them make the mistakes and you know it's so important.

Katie: It hurts to watch him hurt, but at the same time it's nice to see that it hurts him because that means it means something to him. It means he wants to do better. And I'm like, "Okay, I like it." So then he goes to practice on Tuesday and he comes back and he's just bouncing through the door and I'm like, "How did it go?" And he's like, "I didn't make any errors at all. I had a great practice." And then today he comes back from batting and he's like, "My batting coach said that this is the best I've ever hit before." So sometimes when you make mistakes, when you fail, it actually helps you succeed. That is just the way it is. You have to fail to succeed.

Katie: Yes. Every setback I can look back - I have enough life now to look back - I'm like was it a setback or was it a set up? And I love what you said that it's because it means something to him.

Aimee: Yeah. So if you fall down, if you have the setback, taking that knowledge and experience, experiential learning, the experience of doing it, you embody it because it means something to you to show up differently or better the next time.

Katie: Yes. Exactly. It's exactly it.

Aimee: It's just so cool. And for me this is such as I'm doing the work, as I'm doing the podcast, I feel like I learned something from every single guest that applies to my life, either as a CEO or especially as a mom all the time. But you've worked deeply on the people side of it. What have you learned about the intersection of technology and human behavior?

Katie: Oh, I don't know if your podcast is long enough. I can just tell a quick story. What I used to say to Fortune 100 leaders when we're - a lot of times my team would go in and the whole company was doing maybe moving a company's database from on premises to cloud which a lot of organizations have already done that but some were laggards and so we're doing things like that for example.

And what I realized - the technology is eventually going to work but the people may not and almost inevitably every single time when the project was out of scope, out of budget, overtime, whatever. It was a people issue. And I realized that change management isn't about the new software or the technology. It's about understanding that when people resist the change, it's their nervous system in fight, flight, or freeze mode. And so I would have to go in and teach leaders.

There's one of the stories I tell a lot in my keynotes - there was a vice president at Apple who was really upset because he thought I was there to sell like well this project's overtime and over budget so we need more money and I was basically there to sell him on hiring our company to do more. He got really mad at me and he said, "With all due respect, young lady," which wasn't respectful, you think?

Aimee: I'll take the young comment. Thanks for the comment that my wrinkle cream's working.

Katie: But he said, "You act like leadership is rocket science and they either need to get on board or get out of the way." And my nervous system almost went into fight, flight, or freeze mode because that was just so triggering to me. But I took a deep breath and I responded with something very divinely that came down from the heavens because I'd never said it before, but I've said it at least 18 million times since. "With all due respect, sir, I agree, leadership is not rocket science, but it is neuroscience. You know how technology is wired, but you don't know how your people are wired. And if we can meet them where they are, I can guarantee you we will get this project back on track. But right now, it's not the software, it's the people."

That's a story to just sum up that idea of the human behavior and I saw it over and over and over again.

Aimee: Well, we've been a big part of digital transformation, too. And I can - as we're implementing all of this digital transformation for my company with Tom - that was, I didn't even know what it was called when we were doing it as everything was moving to the cloud and all the softwares becoming SaaS and basically this huge transition and transformation happened within the Fortune 500 companies in the US during COVID.

And I wondered myself as I was implementing all of these new technologies - wow, how are they getting these 80,000 employees at this one company on board for this huge technological shift that's happening with these companies. There you are on the human side of it. I never really see the people side of it. I see the engineers, but I never see the employee side. The employees that we've worked with, the ones we can automate things for, of course, they're stoked on that. But I think there's been such a gigantic shift that I can see how the humans could have a really hard time. I mean, I had a hard time switching to cell phones.

Katie: I had a hard time with email.

Aimee: Yeah. This is way bigger. What's been happening is much bigger and happening at much more exponential rate. The technology has been changing so quickly.

Katie: Exponential. That's the right word.

Aimee: Yeah. So, you've said that AI can actually give us more time to be human, and I love that. Could you unpack what that means?

Katie: That's my glass half full approach because I know that others don't feel that way. I think that other people are like, great, we can produce more. But I have a deeply held belief, Amy, that we are human beings, not human doings.

Aimee: I love that.

Katie: Oh, please reuse it. But I get so frustrated because so what? So we can produce more. So we can consume more. What does this mean? And so my glass half full approach is that AI handles the transactional repeatable tasks that drain our energy and that white space that creates - that machines can't replicate or maybe I'll also say that I don't believe we should let them replicate is deep listening, creative problem solving. Now I love to brainstorm with an AI bot, don't get me wrong, or authentic connection and experience. It cannot experience something on your behalf.

All that money that's saved in the organization and that time - send those people on a retreat. Get them back in their bodies. Get them connecting with each other. Get them laughing. Get them telling stories now. So the technology can summarize hundreds of thousands of documents for you. You don't need a human to do that. But when they connect, when they go do a ropes course or they have - they go to one of my keynote labs, wink wink - or they do something human, how is that gonna enrich your organization? So that's my hope for AI.

Aimee: I love that. I was keynoting on Monday and this came up with the audience and it was basically women want to know is AI good or bad and everyone's afraid it's going to take my job. And for me, I had an experience with an employee. We work with the world's second largest food and beverage company. And we walked into the very first meeting and she said, "I'm still hand putting these orders in by hand, these purchase orders." And my VP was like, "Oh, I have a software that we could automate that with." And so we did.

And we were back there a couple years later getting an award. And she stood up and she said, "I now have more" - she had a master's degree - "I have more time and I have more mind share." I was in the middle of writing The Manager at the time. So what I realized was, oh my gosh, for women - because this book started out as the feminist manifesto of the fourth industrial revolution.

Katie: You told me that. I love it.

Aimee: I was just wondering how AI was going to impact women as a tool. In every industrial revolution has given women more freedom and more time and more actually more standing in society. And so I was curious about that and it got me thinking about moms, and as we have more time, what are we going to do with it? 

And what I realized is the one thing AI can't do, it can't give you the experience. And also in business, the reason we were able to get the contract with the second largest food and beverage company in the world was relationships. We had a relationship with a guy who had a relationship with a guy. We have more time to build real IRL communities, online communities. We have more time to spend together and work on our relationships with real people. If we do that, success.

Katie: Yes. What a beautiful reminder about that. Yeah. People prefer to do business with people they trust. And we build trust by spending time together and building relationships.

Aimee: Yes. And we'll have more time for that. I mean, if we're automating our mundane tasks, if I don't have to do the dishes, if my robot can make dinner, maybe I'll have more time to have my friends over. It wouldn't be such a burden to have your neighbor over for dinner at that point. You have more time to socialize and build relationships with the people around you.

Katie: I don't know. It's possible. It is possible. And I think that's one of the things that's so critical for women in my opinion is we have a choice. We have limited time and energy. And we have a choice. We can resist. We can choose to push and be like, "No, no, no." Or we can choose to be a part of the future and co-create it in a way that's meaningful. And so when you say that about the bot doing dishes, if that feels exciting for the women listening, how do we tell Dyson or whoever else is building - that's what we want? We can participate in the creation of the future if we're not spending our time resisting it.

Aimee: If we don't - the thing about the invisible work is only women know what it is. So only women can automate it. So women need to be involved in the creation of the future if we want to automate our mundane tasks. Trust me, a man does not know what we're doing. He wouldn't know how to automate it. So we have to get involved in the automation of our households at the very least. We've got to figure this out.

So this is a call. This is a rise up for the women. Let's join us in the future. Let's get into this technology thing. Let's figure out how we can stop - we've got master's degrees and PhDs and we are folding laundry and we are washing dishes. And this is kind of ridiculous. We could free up our time and our mind share to do higher level tasks in society and within our families. If we didn't have to do all this invisible work that should be visible and therefore automated.

Katie: If it wouldn't break, I would drop my mic right now. Yes.

Aimee: That's what the book was about. The book is like, "Hello girls." I resisted technology, I really did. As a kid, computers were fine. But then the cell phone thing, the internet, I was like, "I just don't know if this is going to catch on." And so when AI came along and we started implementing it in the Fortune 500, I was like, "Wow, what they have is so much cooler than there was really nothing available for consumer level." 

So, as soon as it came along at the consumer level, I'm like, "I have to learn this tool. I have to learn how to use this tool because it's like building a house without a Dremel. Of course you're going to use a power screwdriver and a pneumatic powered nail gun to put a house together. No one's going to use a hammer anymore if you have a better tool. And this is a better tool.

Katie: It's a better tool. And you hit on something that I think bridges our work. I agree. I am there with you. I am in your church. I'm with you. But where I think women specifically struggle in that - well, I can't tell you everything that's in my head and all the steps that I do to get my child ready for school or whatever it is, especially I have friends with neurodivergent children and there's so much mental load on their plates. 

What I've started to notice is this friction because women specifically have over identified with the badge of the burnout. I'm really trying to get away from the word burnout because it's now become so pop culture. But so what I use is toxic resilience because moms are being toxically resilient in this just as much at home in the domestic.

Aimee: Yes. So, let me define that really quick. So, resilience - that's a good thing. Generally we hear the word resilient and people generally have a positive orientation to it. But just like positivity there's toxic positivity, toxic masculinity. What I came to understand - this was the medicine I built for myself. This is the work I did for myself after that boss was I realized I had become toxically resilient. 

Meaning I wore as a badge that I could push through, bounce back, work harder, and get it done. So, I wired my brain literally to work so much harder and push and prove - people pleasing and overcommitting my time, saying no when my body said yes when my body said no. And if you look up the word resilience or resilient on dictionary.com or whatever, one of the things that it'll tell you, one of the phrases is to bounce back to its shape. 

And I started to realize that yeah, I would work my butt off, but I was going back. So I was basically getting through something, but I wasn't growing through it. It was almost like, oh, something bad happens. Hold my breath. Work really hard. Okay, I'm going to go back to this homeostasis that didn't exist. 

The opposite of toxic resilience that I set up in my work is regeneration. The idea that you regenerate. You actually let that break and compost that into building something new. But I think it's really important for people to understand that toxic resilience - the voice in your head, the toxic resilient voice is work harder, be more perfect, control more things. And so that's where I'm saying what I see the pattern in women is well if I give up this invisible work who am I?

One of my sister-in-laws when I was writing the book - I had been talking to another CEO who worked at AI, a woman, a young woman and she said well all that stuff's going to get automated by 2030. And so I was talking to my sister-in-law about it. And I'm like well what are we going to do when we have some bot to wash our dishes? What's next for us? What do we do with our extra time? We can't sit around and drink Chardonnay. I'm not going to.

And she was very upset she's like I like washing the dishes and I'm like okay and there's going to be some resistance to - and there you have it. I don't know what that is but it is a thing. I was like okay and sometimes I like washing the dishes too. I just don't want to do them when I've been at meetings all day and I'm exhausted and the house is torn apart by the seven people that live there.

Katie: Yeah. It's so interesting. There is something cathartic about it, right? Back to experience. But I just wonder about in that example, what's the story underneath it? What are you scared - I find that more women are scared of their potential than scared of failure. We have insane potential.

Aimee: I see that. There's so much divinity in women and for women to be in our full authenticity, fully expressed selves with this divinity and masculinity, we can't always be pushing and we can't always be cycling through and feeling. But when there's a balance and I think we've just had the pendulum swing has just been so far in the produce, produce, produce, produce. Very yang, not very yin. No rest.

Katie: No rest.

Aimee: No rest. All work. I'm kind of hoping we can swing back. I hope that the fourth industrial revolution actually pushes us back into some sort of more place of stasis for humanity where we have a better balance between work and play and rest and work and masculine and feminine. And I really hope that we move more towards an egalitarian society.

Katie: Me too. That would be very nice. That's why we hit it off for sure.

Katie: There's lots of things but just some of the easy administrative stuff is it can be its own assistant. Or I'll brainstorm, too. I'll put things that I've written in a whole body of work, but I never just accept it the way it is. I still bring my embodied intelligence. I still bring my intuition. I think that's really, really critical to share.

Aimee: Yeah, it really gives you a good first draft, but you got to go through and put a little human on it, right? Give it a little bit of your own flavor.

Katie: Yes. Funny little example. I'm also my son's soccer coach. So, if I just took what AI told me, we would have had a wrestling match instead of a soccer match. But it saved me so much time in thinking about a fun practice plan. And then it was like, oh, I can switch out these two kids. Let me do a gut check. Yep, that's a better starting lineup. So, I did that in less than four minutes. Three minutes. Whereas 3 years ago, I was also his soccer coach. That would have taken me almost an hour because I would have really poured over it.

Aimee: Yes. Oh, I know. It takes an hour. I'm like, "Oh." He's like, "Teach me how to use AI." I'm like, "Okay, I'll teach you."

Katie: Exactly. Come on, hubby baseball.

Aimee: So, what would you say to leaders who fear that technology will make their people less human or even less necessary? I hear it a lot.

Katie: So a little tough love is if it's the person who fears of being replaced. I think - what is your unique genius? What lights you up? Start thinking about that instead of resisting it. Think about okay, AI is going to take my job - and the way that I would tell my leader that I'm going to use - I'm going to use AI in this way so that now I can - and I think that's a really powerful phrase. I'm going to use AI, we're going to use AI so that now I can.

I try to - a little tough love but that empowerment of instead of it's happening to you, that sort of victim mentality - what is in your control? Where can you get some agency?

Aimee: Yeah, absolutely. I couldn't agree more. So you're writing a book on toxic resilience. I'm so proud of you. What inspired the topic? We kind of went into that a little bit and what does it mean to you? I guess really this is your first book, right?

Katie: Well, I did write a 267 page dissertation but I wouldn't even recommend reading it. So, yeah. Yes.

That part takes energy and time before even actually putting pen to paper.

Katie: Yeah. Thank you for that. And I appreciate you sharing and validating that that was something for you too. I think a lot of people feel it, but I would imagine especially women. Aimee: Thank you. Katie: I know we are limitless. We are just one with source. And so if you can actually check that ego and let your brilliance flow. And the other thing too, Amy, it's like - no one's lived my story. I have a battle with infertility. I actually just picked my phone up and started talking it into my notes because it was just pouring out of me whenever I would get in the car alone, it was there and I would just talk it into the notes of my phone.
